maps.angle: Pickleball/racket-sport video analysis products as of mid-2026: pb.vision, SwingVision, and NEW entrants since ~July 2026; what they ship (3D? coaching? live?), pricing, accuracy claims, processing latency claims; what differentiates our full 3D world + trust-band approach.

findings.claim: PB Vision pricing: Starter $20/mo, Premium $50/mo, custom Professional tier; offers '3D trajectory analysis' of shots (drops/drives/lobs) from uploaded match footage, plus shot-type/rally/positioning stats and auto-highlights. Also partnered with DUPR to feed AI video analysis into player ratings.
findings.relevance_to_us: Confirms pb.vision's '3D' claim is trajectory-level (ball path per shot type), not a full 3D body+court+ball world reconstruction — supports our differentiation message that we build a full 3D scene, not just shot-trajectory overlays. Also flags DUPR-rating integration as a distribution channel we don't have.

findings.claim: SwingVision AI Coaching is bundled in the Pro subscription (30 recording hours/month) and gives personalized post-session coaching advice; recent pickleball-specific updates add rally-scoring alerts during returner freezes and ball-machine/serve-practice modes.
findings.relevance_to_us: SwingVision already ships single-camera LLM-ish 'coaching advice after each session' — i.e. a competitor is already in the coaching-output space we're about to enter (3-stage grounded coach). Their coaching appears stat-summary based, not obviously grounded/fabrication-audited; our 0-fabrication-bar + trust bands is a real differentiator if we can substantiate it publicly.

findings.claim: NEW entrant: SwingVantage 'Motion Lab' — free phone-video-only 3D swing reconstruction (spin/slow/step-through) with phase scores (power, rotation, balance, sequencing, timing, consistency), pickleball-specific engine (compact stroke, kitchen, third-shot drop), core Motion Lab tier is free with optional paid AI upgrade.
findings.relevance_to_us: This is the closest existing product to our '3D world' promise for individual swing biomechanics — a free single-phone 3D body-motion product already exists. This is a load-bearing competitive signal: our 3D differentiation claim needs to be sharper than 'we do 3D' (they already do body-in-3D free) — likely needs to rest on full-scene (ball+court+multi-player) fidelity, trust/confidence bands, and coaching grounding rather than 3D-ness alone.

findings.claim: NEW entrant: Wingfield — hardware+software 'AI Pro'/'Vision Pro'/'Cam+' smart-court camera systems for tennis/padel/pickleball, ~€6,999 one-time hardware (or ~€187/mo financing over 48mo) plus a separate ongoing software/service fee; gamified drills, live streaming, on-court video challenges.
findings.relevance_to_us: Different business model (fixed installed hardware vs. our phone-video pipeline) — informs pricing anchor if we ever consider a venue/club hardware SKU, but not a direct threat to phone-based product; no explicit full-3D-world or coaching-with-trust-bands claim found.

findings.claim: NEW entrant/partnership: PlayReplay (via Pickleball Inc. as exclusive worldwide distributor) — 4-camera non-intrusive automated line-calling system with ITF Silver Classification for indoor/outdoor accuracy, 100M+ line calls logged across 250k+ matches; also does ball speed/spin/shot-type analytics; debuting at PPA/MLP events in 2026, later expanding to clubs/rec/amateur.
findings.relevance_to_us: Officiating-focused (line calls), not coaching/3D-world — different market segment, but their claimed spin/speed detection at pro-tour data scale (250k+ matches) is a benchmark for what 'good' ball physics data volume looks like versus our current 1,121 owner-reviewed frames; also a potential future data-partnership or acquisition target for ball-tracking ground truth.

findings.claim: NEW entrant: Owl AI + Major League Pickleball — first AI officiating system powered entirely by existing broadcast cameras + cloud AI (no proprietary hardware/sensors), live as of MLP's 2026 Dallas season start; used for line calls and challenge-system turnaround alongside human referees. No published latency numbers.
findings.relevance_to_us: Officiating/pro-broadcast segment, not consumer coaching — low direct relevance to our roadmap, but shows the 'software-only, existing-camera' framing is winning positioning language in pickleball tech PR generally (useful messaging parallel: we do full 3D from a single consumer phone, no fixed install).

dead_ends: Could not find published processing-latency figures (e.g. seconds/minute-of-video) for pb.vision, SwingVision, Wingfield, PlayReplay, or Owl AI in this pass -- all sources describe 'fast turnaround' qualitatively only.

No public accuracy-claim numbers (e.g. F1/px error) found for any competitor's ball-tracking or line-calling beyond PlayReplay's ITF Silver Classification and '100M+ calls' scale claim -- no comparable px-level metric to line up against our F1@20px bar.

Did not find any competitor publicly claiming a full multi-body + ball + court joint 3D scene reconstruction (our core claim) -- SwingVantage's Motion Lab is the closest but appears to be single-player body-only, not a joint world with trust/confidence bands.

No new entrants found on court/ball 3D specifically since our last 2026-07-05 survey beyond SwingVantage, Wingfield, PlayReplay, Owl AI -- did not turn up any stealth/YC-style startup announcements this pass; a search on Twitter/X or Product Hunt might surface earlier-stage entrants not yet indexed by web search.

maps.angle: Automated sports coaching feedback + LLM grounding 2025-2026: systems generating coaching from quantified metrics with fabrication control; rubric/eval benchmarks for feedback quality; retrieval/comparator architectures constraining LLM outputs to verified facts.

findings.claim: BioCoach: a 3-stage pipeline (exercise-specific degree-of-freedom selector -> structured biomechanical context -> vision-biomechanics-conditioned feedback module) fuses 3D skeletal kinematics + video to produce grounded exercise coaching text, explicitly designed so the LLM cannot generate claims unsupported by extracted pose/biomechanics evidence.
findings.relevance_to_us: Near-identical architectural shape to our planned 3-stage grounded coach (deterministic features -> rule comparator -> format-locked LLM). Worth reading their DOF-selector + structured-context design for how they scope what the LLM is allowed to say per exercise/movement, and whether their approach to 'structured biomechanical context' generalizes to a rule-comparator like ours.

findings.claim: BioCoach is evaluated on QEVD-bio-fit-coach, a biomechanics-augmented extension of the QEVD-fit-coach benchmark (74 videos, 23 exercises, ~2,484 timestamped feedback instances), using a new LLM-as-judge metric 'LLM-Bio-Acc' that scores biomechanical correctness and specificity of generated feedback (alongside standard NLG metrics).
findings.relevance_to_us: Gives us a candidate eval methodology/metric pattern (LLM-judge scored for biomechanical correctness + specificity) we could adapt for our 0-fabrication coaching bar instead of inventing an eval protocol from scratch.

findings.claim: A CHI 2026 survey/position paper on next-gen motion-coaching LLM systems documents that LLMs 'hallucinate often, providing feedback that was completely irrelevant to the sport and giving fake information, resulting in worse performances compared to human coaches' when not properly grounded, and proposes design requirements including persistent athlete models with explicit guardrails and multimodal fused sensing.
findings.relevance_to_us: Independent confirmation that our 0-fabrication design bar is the correct priority, not overcaution -- ungrounded LLM sports feedback measurably underperforms human coaches per this survey. Supports the frozen architectural choice of comparator-before-LLM.

findings.claim: RubricRAG (arXiv 2603.20882, ~2026-03) proposes retrieving domain-knowledge-grounded rubrics to constrain/improve LLM evaluation and feedback generation, an interpretable/reliable alternative to free-form LLM judging.
findings.relevance_to_us: Potential technique for our rule-comparator stage or for building a verification/eval harness on top of the coach output -- retrieval-grounded rubrics as an alternative or complement to hand-written comparator rules.

findings.claim: Scoping review of LLM-based exercise/health coaching evaluation strategies finds existing LLM benchmarks are 'ill-equipped' for exercise-coaching's unique demands and that no existing framework evaluates an AI's ability to give safe real-time corrective form feedback or adapt coaching dynamically.
findings.relevance_to_us: Confirms there is no established eval standard for our exact use case (real-time corrective coaching) -- we likely need to build our own eval harness (as planned) rather than adopt an off-the-shelf benchmark; QEVD-bio-fit-coach/LLM-Bio-Acc above is the closest existing candidate to borrow from.

dead_ends: General RAG hallucination-mitigation literature (grounded summarization hitting 0.7-1.5% hallucination rates in 2025) is generic factuality-RAG work, not sports/coaching-specific -- confirms our approach direction but has no sport-specific technique to borrow.

AgentCoach (CHI 2026), SoleCoach (CHI 2026), BoxingPro (IoT-LLM boxing coach), GPTCoach (physical activity coaching) are all motivational/adherence or wearable-sensor coaching systems, not vision/3D-pose-grounded technique coaching -- adjacent but not a closer match than BioCoach; not pursued further.

Rubric-generation-for-LLM-judges papers (Auto-Rubric, OptimSyn, FeedEval, iRULER) are general NLG/education-essay-feedback grounding work, not sports-specific -- noted RubricRAG as the one worth banking, rest are dead ends for our angle.

maps.angle: Small/fast object detection+tracking in sports video, 2025-2026 SOTA: architectures beyond heatmap CNNs (WASB/TrackNet class) — transformers, motion-centric, temporal fusion, event-inspired; concrete benchmark numbers on racquet-sport ball datasets and what beats WASB-class F1 at what runtime cost.

findings.claim: TrackNetV4 (ICASSP 2025) adds learnable 'motion attention maps' from frame-differencing, fused with visual features via a motion-aware fusion mechanism, specifically to fix TrackNetV2/V3 failures under partial occlusion/low visibility on tennis-ball and shuttlecock datasets. It is a drop-in enhancement layer on top of existing TrackNet backbones, not a from-scratch replacement.
findings.relevance_to_us: Direct architectural idea we could port onto our WASB/seed-student stack cheaply: an explicit motion-difference channel might reduce the class of hard frames (fast/blurred ball) that are currently driving CVAT disagreement-queue volume, without a full retrain-from-scratch.

findings.claim: TrackNetV5 (arXiv Dec 2025, 'Residual-Driven Spatio-Temporal Refinement and Motion Direction Decoupling') reports a new SOTA on the TrackNetV2 badminton dataset: F1 0.9859, accuracy 0.9733 — beating prior TrackNet versions on that specific benchmark.
findings.relevance_to_us: Newer than our known WASB/TrackNetV2-V3 baseline and posts numbers that, on paper, exceed our held-out bar's ballpark — but it's a same-domain (in-distribution badminton) benchmark, not a cross-domain zero-shot number like our WASB-on-pickleball setup, so it doesn't directly falsify our F1@20px=0.7248 ceiling. Worth a scoped eval only if we get GPU slack; not urgent.

findings.claim: TOTNet (arXiv Aug 2025, published Computer Vision and Image Understanding Feb 2026) is an occlusion-aware temporal network using 3D convs + visibility-weighted loss + occlusion augmentation, evaluated across 4 racquet-sport datasets (tennis, badminton, table tennis) plus a new Paralympic table-tennis occlusion dataset (TTA, 9,159 frames/1,996 occlusion cases). Reports RMSE 37.30→7.19 and fully-occluded-frame accuracy 0.63→0.80 vs prior SOTA. No runtime/latency numbers published; code at github.com/AugustRushG/TOTNet.
findings.relevance_to_us: Occlusion handling (ball behind paddle/net/body) is exactly the failure mode implicated in some of our internal→held-out inversions. TOTNet's occlusion-augmentation + visibility-weighted loss training recipe is a candidate technique to fold into our own seed-student training even if we don't adopt its full architecture — flagged as load-bearing because it's a training-recipe idea, not just an architecture swap, and directly targets a known weak spot.

findings.claim: An event-based active-vision spin-estimation system (arXiv, 'Event-based Gaze Control System for Accurate Real-time Spin Estimation in Professional Ball Games') pairs an event camera with pan/tilt galvanometer mirrors and a focus-tunable telephoto lens to track and spin-estimate unmodified balls across table tennis/baseball/tennis/golf. Reports 2.1% magnitude / 4.0° axis error on static balls; in live 3-view professional table-tennis matches: 8.8% magnitude / 6.4° axis error at 3ms latency, 750Hz throughput.
findings.relevance_to_us: Directly adjacent to our just-killed scalar-Magnus-spin lane. This is a hardware-dependent (event camera + actuated optics) solution achieving spin accuracy far beyond what our RGB-only reprojection approach can reach — confirms our kill was likely correct given commodity-camera noise floors, and shows the actual unlock for spin requires a sensing upgrade we don't have, not a better solver. Useful evidence for why we gated spin on 'per-segment view-geometry confidence' rather than chasing more model complexity.

findings.claim: RF-DETR (Roboflow, ICLR 2026), a DINOv2-backbone real-time detection transformer, is being used directly for pickleball in a May 2026 Roboflow tutorial ('Automate Pickleball Analytics with Roboflow'): single-frame detect-then-reason (RF-DETR detection + Claude Sonnet 4.5 tactical commentary), reporting 76.6% mAP@50, 84.5% precision, 77.3% recall, 80.7% F1 on their pickleball detection set. No multi-frame tracking, no ball-trajectory work, no comparison to WASB/TrackNet.
findings.relevance_to_us: A named competitor/adjacent product building pickleball CV tooling in public with an LLM-commentary layer conceptually similar to our planned 3-stage grounded coach. Their numbers (80.7% F1 on general pickleball object detection, not small-ball-only) are well below our ball-specific bar and they explicitly haven't tackled tracking/trajectory — so no threat to our ball-chain priority, but worth knowing this exists when scoping the coaching-stage competitive landscape.

findings.claim: RF-DETR itself (open-source, ICLR 2026) is marketed as SOTA on COCO/RF100-VL with latency low enough for real-time video, and is being paired with OC-SORT for small/fast-object tracking (e.g. maintaining ball identity above 160km/h in other racquet-sport tutorials).
findings.relevance_to_us: A candidate general-purpose detector backbone alternative to heatmap-CNN WASB-class models if we ever revisit the detector architecture question, but no published apples-to-apples F1@20px number on a tennis/pickleball ball dataset exists yet — not a proven beat of our bar, just a plausible candidate to bench later.

dead_ends: paperswithcode.com sports-ball-detection-and-tracking-on-tennis leaderboard page redirected to an unrelated huggingface trending-papers page and could not be fetched directly — no independent leaderboard confirmation obtained beyond what's cited in TrackNetV4/V5 and TOTNet papers themselves.

General searches for a '2026 model that beats WASB F1' turned up no paper explicitly benchmarking against WASB-SBDT by name; TrackNetV5/TOTNet numbers are same-domain (in-distribution, non-pickleball) benchmarks, not proof against our zero-shot cross-domain setup.

No table-tennis/badminton/tennis paper found that reports results specifically on a pickleball dataset or claims cross-sport zero-shot transfer comparable to our WASB-zero-shot setup — the domain-transfer angle we care about most remains unaddressed in the literature.

maps.angle: Label-efficient domain adaptation for detection 2025-2026: is disagreement/error-mix active-learning sample selection validated? Semi/self-supervised adaptation with small human-verified sets (500-20k frames), pseudo-label reliability under domain shift, leave-one-domain-out generalization, and shape of the labels-vs-accuracy curve.

findings.claim: A Jan 2026 practical-insights study benchmarking MixPL/Semi-DETR/Consistent-Teacher across k-shot settings (1-150 labeled images/category) finds the labels-vs-accuracy curve is steep from 1→100 shots then flattens; 'structured per-class supervision yields stronger performance than percentage-based sampling' (150 per-class shots beat a 5%-of-dataset baseline); a specialized ('Beetle') domain plateaus by ~100 shots/class, i.e. small specialized domains saturate fast.
findings.relevance_to_us: Directly bears on our labels-vs-accuracy roadmap: our own curve (486 rows already beating tennis-control and 61k-warm-start on hard frames) still climbing toward 10-20k target — this paper's evidence says most SSOD methods plateau far earlier (~100-150/class) in a specialized domain, so either (a) our task is harder / more diverse than 'Beetle'-style single-domain SSOD and genuinely needs more labels, or (b) we should explicitly test whether returns are already flattening around 1-5k and redirect surplus labeling budget elsewhere. Worth an internal ablation checkpoint at 1k/3k/6k/10k rows.

findings.claim: 'One-Shot Badminton Shuttle Detection for Mobile Robots' (arXiv 2603.06691, submitted Mar 4 2026, revised Mar 17 2026) fine-tunes YOLOv8 on 20,510 semi-automatically annotated frames across 11 backgrounds for egocentric shuttlecock detection, reaching F1=0.86 in similar-to-training environments but only F1=0.70 in entirely unseen environments — a ~16pt drop attributed to shuttle size and background-texture complexity shift.
findings.relevance_to_us: Near-exact structural analog to our ball-chain problem: same order of magnitude on labeled frames (20k target matches our 10-20k target), same racket-sport tiny-fast-object domain, and the SAME internal-to-held-out generalization gap phenomenon we've hit (our 4 recorded inversions, F1@20px 0.7248 bar). This is external validation that a ~15-20pt in-domain-vs-novel-domain F1 gap is normal/expected at this label scale for small racket-sport objects, not a sign our pipeline is uniquely broken — useful calibration for how much more held-out generalization to expect from scaling 1.1k→10-20k labels.

findings.claim: Classification Committee for Active Deep Object Detection (arXiv 2308.08476) formalizes query-by-committee for detection, arguing classification-disagreement alone under-captures true committee disagreement in multi-task (cls+box) detectors and that localization disagreement must be modeled separately/jointly for effective active-learning sample selection.
findings.relevance_to_us: Not new (pre-2025) but directly validates the mechanism behind our CVAT disagreement-queue (large-offset/student-only/teacher-only mix): the paper's finding that localization disagreement (offset) is a necessary complement to pure classification/confidence disagreement matches our 'large-offset' bucket design. Confirms our selection heuristic isn't ad hoc — it mirrors a peer-reviewed committee-disagreement AL formulation for detection.

findings.claim: A Jan/Sept 2025 line of work (Scale-Consistent and Temporally Ensembled UDA, PMC11723150) reports that confidence-only pseudo-label selection causes poor localization for small objects under domain shift, and proposes combining temporal localization stability with classification confidence (TEPLS) plus cross-scale prediction consistency to filter noisy pseudo-labels.
findings.relevance_to_us: Relevant to pseudo-label reliability under domain shift for a small fast-moving object (their motivating case is essentially our regime: small objects, confidence miscalibration). Suggests a concrete lever if/when we resume self-training the ball student: gate pseudo-labels by cross-frame/temporal localization stability, not classifier confidence alone — could be a candidate follow-up to strengthen the seed-trained-student pipeline beyond disagreement-queue labeling alone.

findings.claim: 'De-Simplifying Pseudo Labels' (DeSimPL, arXiv 2507.00608, July 2025) identifies 'simple-label bias' — self-labeling pipelines systematically favor easy/simple pseudo-labels — as a central cause of performance ceilings in domain-adaptive object detection, and proposes explicitly countering this bias rather than just raising confidence thresholds.
findings.relevance_to_us: A plausible mechanistic explanation for why our raw-WASB-tennis-ckpt zero-shot (and the 61k public warm-start) underperform the small seed-trained student on HARD frames specifically: naive self-training / large public corpora over-represent easy/simple ball appearances, while our owner-labeled disagreement queue is explicitly adversarial/hard-mined. Supports the wave-5 owner ruling (raw WASB seed blessed over 2D-teacher plan) as consistent with this literature, and argues our hard-frame-biased CVAT queue is the right lever, not incidental.

dead_ends: Searched for 'active learning disagreement sampling object detection domain adaptation 2026' directly — surfaced mostly pre-2025 general-AL background (uncertainty sampling, query-by-committee basics) and adjacent-but-not-matching hits (Bi3D 3D detection, DELTA graph domain adaptation, source-free ADA) with no strong 2026 racket/ball-sport-specific disagreement-AL result.

TrackNet lineage search returned only pre-2025 fine-tuning-with-5/10/20-images anecdotes and TrackNetV4 (Sept 2024, already effectively 'known' as v2/v3 lineage) — no new 2025-2026 TrackNet-family domain-adaptation paper found.

Attempted WebFetch on arXiv PDF 2409.09412 ('Label Convergence: Defining an Upper Performance Bound... through Contradictory Annotations') — potentially relevant to our F1@20px ceiling/inversions phenomenon — but the PDF fetch returned corrupted/unparseable binary content; would need a retry via the abstract/HTML page (arxiv.org/abs/2409.09412) instead of the raw PDF.

Leave-one-domain-out generalization search returned only generic PACS/OfficeHome/DomainNet domain-generalization benchmark papers (CAT semi-supervised-DG, Face4FairShifts, etc.) with no sports/ball-tracking-specific LOSO result newer or more specific than our own standing LoSO validation practice.

maps.angle: Ball spin + trajectory estimation from monocular sports video 2024-2026: when is Magnus/spin identifiable (view geometry, segment length, fps), recent drag+Magnus ODE fits, and conditions where adding spin improves vs. absorbs noise in reprojection.

findings.claim: TT3D (table tennis 3D reconstruction) fits a monocular ball-flight ODE with explicit drag (kD=3.8e-4 kg/s^4) and Magnus (kM=4.86e-6 kg·s^4·m^-1) coefficients, but critically estimates spin by optimizing velocity+spin at the BOUNCE (post-bounce direction change), not by fitting pure free-flight curvature, and requires the ball visible for >=3 frames after a bounce for the trajectory-segmentation/spin-inference step to work.
findings.relevance_to_us: Directly bears on our killed Magnus experiment: we only tried spin-on flight-segment reprojection RMSE (killed, 2/3 clips worse at lambda=0.05, >=8-inlier gate). TT3D's identifiability signal comes from bounce-induced trajectory kinks, a completely different, likely stronger cue than in-flight curvature alone at our detector noise floor — pickleball has frequent bounces/paddle contacts, so a bounce-anchored spin estimator (rather than free-flight-only) may be the unlock our per-segment view-geometry-confidence gate is waiting for.

findings.claim: "Towards Ball Spin and Trajectory Analysis in Table Tennis Broadcast Videos via Physically Grounded Synthetic-to-Real Transfer" (CVPRW 2025) trains a network purely on physically-correct synthetic data and achieves 92.0% spin-class accuracy and 2D reprojection error of 0.19% of image diagonal on real monocular broadcast video, with zero real spin labels used in training.
findings.relevance_to_us: Different, newer name not in our known list. Suggests a synthetic-to-real learned-spin-classifier path as an alternative to our house physics solver (scipy TRF/huber) — could sidestep the noise-floor problem we hit, since it never explicitly fits the Magnus ODE via least squares on noisy detections; worth a light scouting look before our next spin revisit, not urgent.

findings.claim: "Uplifting Table Tennis" (Nov 2025) proposes a two-stage pipeline: front-end 2D perception trained with abundant real 2D labels, back-end synthetic-only 2D-to-3D-and-spin uplifting network engineered for robustness to missing detections and varying fps, explicitly built to generalize zero-shot from synthetic to noisy real detections.
findings.relevance_to_us: Newer name (not WASB/TrackNet/BlurBall/MonoTrack/etc.), and its explicit design goal — robustness to real-world detection noise and variable fps for the uplift-to-spin step — is the same failure mode (detector noise) that killed our Magnus attempt. Architecture (synthetic-only uplift net decoupled from real 2D front-end) is a candidate design pattern if we revisit spin, but paper's PDF text wasn't extractable for deeper technical detail (worth a follow-up read).

findings.claim: "Where Is The Ball: 3D Ball Trajectory Estimation From 2D Monocular Tracking" (LSTM canonical-3D-representation pipeline) explicitly does NOT model spin/Magnus at all, stating aerodynamics/spin are left to future work, yet reports 87.21% landing accuracy on tennis (vs 53.35% for a synthetic-data baseline SynthNet) using only gravity + ground-plane constraints and known camera params.
findings.relevance_to_us: Corroborates our own finding indirectly: a no-spin monocular trajectory model can already get strong landing/position accuracy without touching Magnus, i.e. spin may be a separable, later-stage refinement rather than a prerequisite for core trajectory accuracy — supports our frozen ruling to keep scalar-spin-only and gate it rather than making it load-bearing for base trajectory work.

findings.claim: TT4D (ACM MM 2026 submission, monocular broadcast pipeline+dataset) infers spin only implicitly via a synthetic-trained lifting network using Rotary Positional Embeddings keyed to exact timestamps specifically to handle varying/irregular frame rates — it provides no formal characterization of fps/segment-length thresholds for spin identifiability, and has no real spin ground truth (spin is model output only, not annotated).
findings.relevance_to_us: Confirms the field still lacks a formal identifiability characterization (fps/segment-length/view-geometry thresholds) for monocular spin — same gap we're gated on with our own per-segment view-geometry-confidence criterion. No existing paper hands us a ready-made threshold; we likely have to derive our own gate empirically, which validates continuing our current gated/revisit-later approach rather than searching for an external formula.

dead_ends: Direct WebFetch of arXiv PDF binaries (2511.20250, 2504.19863 pdf variant) failed — tool could not parse encoded PDF streams; had to rely on the arxiv.org/abs or /html mirror pages instead, so some quantitative ablation details (e.g. Uplifting Table Tennis' actual spin-vs-no-spin reprojection numbers) remain unconfirmed and would need a direct paper read.

Searched explicitly for 'spin reprojection error tennis Magnus ODE least squares 2025' hoping for a paper with a direct spin-on-vs-spin-off reprojection ablation matching our exact experiment shape — none of the returned papers report that specific ablation; TT3D comes closest but doesn't quantify it either.

Event-based gaze control spin estimation paper (arxiv 2606.26780) surfaced in first search but not pursued further — it uses event cameras (specialized hardware), not standard monocular RGB video, so likely out of scope for our smartphone-video pipeline; flagged but not deep-dived given time budget.

other.reason: Verified against primary sources: arxiv.org/abs/2606.26780v1 (and cross-confirmed via WebSearch/arxiv.org/pdf/2606.26780 and arxiv.org/html/2606.26780v1). Title matches exactly: 'Event-based Gaze Control System for Accurate Real-time Spin Estimation in Professional Ball Games' (authors Yunpu Hu et al.). Submitted June 25, 2026 (v1) -- consistent with the claimed 2026-06 date. System description matches: event camera + high-speed pan/tilt galvanometer mirrors + low-latency focus-tunable telephoto lens, tracking unmodified balls across table tennis/baseball/tennis/golf. Both metrics match exactly: static-ball offline method = 2.1% magnitude error / 4.0 degrees axis error; live 3-view professional table-tennis matches (online method) = 8.8% magnitude / 6.4 degrees axis mismatch, 3ms latency, 750Hz throughput. No newer paper was found in search results that supersedes or contradicts these numbers (a v2 exists dated June 30, 2026, but no evidence surfaced that it revises the headline metrics).

Verified directly against the primary source (arXiv abstract page and full HTML text at arxiv.org/abs/2606.26780v1 and arxiv.org/html/2606.26780v1). The paper is titled exactly "Event-based Gaze Control System for Accurate Real-time Spin Estimation in Professional Ball Games," authored by Yunpu Hu, Fabian Schilling, Valentina Cavinato, Asude Aydin, Agis Politis, Ricardo Tapiador Morales, Kirk Y.W. Scheper, Peter Dürr, Naoya Takahashi, submitted 2026-06-25 (v1) — matching the claimed 2026-06 date. It confirms: an event camera paired with high-speed pan/tilt galvanometer mirrors and a focus-tunable telephoto lens; tracking/spin-estimating unmodified balls across table tennis, baseball, tennis, and golf (static-ball offline experiments); mean magnitude/axis errors of 2.1%/4.0° on static balls; and in live professional table-tennis matches using a multi-view (three-view, per Section 3.1.2, fusing per-view estimates) setup, 8.8% magnitude / 6.4° axis mismatch at 3ms latency and 750Hz throughput. No newer contradicting result was found; this appears to be the paper's only version referenced (v1/v2 both from June 2026, consistent with the cited date). All specific figures and technical claims in the statement are directly corroborated by the primary source.

Most of the claim checks out against the primary source (arxiv.org/abs/2508.09650, HTML at arxiv.org/html/2508.09650): title, authors, 3D-conv + visibility-weighted-loss + occlusion-augmentation design, evaluation across 4 racquet-sport datasets (tennis, badminton, table tennis) plus the new Paralympic TTA dataset (9,159 samples/1,996 occlusion cases), the RMSE 37.30->7.19 and fully-occluded-frame accuracy 0.63->0.80 figures, the journal republication (confirmed via ScienceDirect: Computer Vision and Image Understanding, Vol. 264, Feb 2026, article 104657), and the code repo at github.com/AugustRushG/TOTNet all check out. However, the specific sub-claim 'No runtime/latency numbers published' is false and refutes the claim as stated: the paper's Table 2 explicitly reports FPS figures (TOTNet: 8.65M params / 28.08 FPS; TOTNet(OF): 8.66M params / 12.19 FPS), and the paper explicitly discusses inference speed, stating TOTNet 'is explicitly designed for offline sports analytics applications ... where inference speed is less critical than accurate and robust tracking' — i.e., speed was measured and discussed, just deprioritized as a design goal, not omitted from the paper.
other.corrected_claim: TOTNet (arXiv:2508.09650, Aug 2025; journal version published in Computer Vision and Image Understanding, Vol. 264, Feb 2026, article 104657) is an occlusion-aware temporal network using 3D convs + visibility-weighted loss + occlusion augmentation, evaluated across 4 racquet-sport datasets (tennis, badminton, table tennis) plus a new Paralympic table-tennis occlusion dataset (TTA, 9,159 frames/1,996 occlusion cases). Reports RMSE 37.30->7.19 and fully-occluded-frame accuracy 0.63->0.80 (on the TTA fully-occluded subset) vs prior SOTA. The paper DOES publish runtime numbers: Table 2 reports ~28.08 FPS for TOTNet and ~12.19 FPS for the TOTNet(OF) variant (8.65-8.66M params), with the authors noting the design prioritizes offline-analysis accuracy over inference speed. Code at github.com/AugustRushG/TOTNet.

other.corrected_claim: TOTNet (arXiv:2508.09650, submitted Aug 2025; journal version published in Computer Vision and Image Understanding, DOI 10.1016/j.cviu.2026.104657) is an occlusion-aware temporal ball-tracking network (3D convs + visibility-weighted loss + occlusion augmentation) evaluated on four datasets spanning tennis, badminton, and table tennis — one of which is the new Paralympic table-tennis occlusion dataset TTA (9,159 samples, 1,996 occlusion cases) introduced in this paper, not a fifth dataset in addition to the four. other.reason: Confirmed against the primary source (arXiv:2504.10035v2, "TT3D: Table Tennis 3D Reconstruction," Gossard/Ziegler/Zell — v1 submitted 2025-04-14, v2 revised 2025-06-25, matching the claimed date). The physics ODE and both coefficients are verbatim in the paper: m·v̇ = kD‖v‖v (drag) + kM(ω×v) (Magnus) + mg, with kD = 3.8e-4 kg·s⁻⁴ and kM = 4.86e-6 kg·s⁻⁴·m⁻¹ (Section 5.1). The paper's optimization (Section 5.2) solves for pre-bounce velocity/spin [v⁻, ω⁻] by minimizing reprojection error across the whole segment, and the bounce model (Eqs. 7-10) relates pre/post-bounce spin/velocity — the paper itself frames the bounce direction-change as what makes spin inference from monocular 2D tracking possible/refined, not the pure free-flight curvature alone (consistent with the paper's abstract claim that it infers spin "without relying on human pose estimation or racket tracking" by exploiting the bounce). The 3-frames-after-bounce requirement is stated verbatim in the Limitations section: "our segmentation approach relies on second-degree polynomials, requiring the ball to be visible for at least three frames after a bounce." One minor nuance: the paper's language suggests curvature gives an initial/coarse spin signal that the bounce-based optimization then refines/disambiguates, rather than the bounce being used in total isolation from curvature — but this is a shading distinction, not a factual contradiction of the claim as stated. Searched for newer contradicting work: found TT4D (arXiv:2605.01234, 2026-05), which proposes an alternative learned-lifting pipeline that avoids segmentation-first bounce-dependent processing and claims better robustness to occlusion/unreliable detections — but this is a newer/competing method, not a source that disputes what TT3D's own paper says about itself. No primary source contradicts the specific technical facts asserted in the claim.

syntheses:
# Cited Synthesis — Ball Chain / Coaching SOTA Scan (2026-07-08)

Scope note: only claims that were adversarially refuted **and returned `survives:true`** are marked **[CORROBORATED]** below. Everything else — including findings that were never run through refute, and findings that were refuted with `survives:false` even where most of the underlying facts checked out — is labeled **UNVERIFIED**, per instruction. Two findings (TrackNetV5, TOTNet) fall in a middle case: the adversarial pass confirmed most of the claim but caught a specific factual error, so I report the **corrected** version and still tag it UNVERIFIED overall since `survives:false`.

---

## (1) WHAT CHANGES OUR PLAN

**1a. Labels-vs-accuracy curves for specialized domains plateau early (~100–150 shots/class) in published SSOD benchmarks — we have not checked whether ours already has.**
[CORROBORATED] — arxiv.org/html/2601.13380 (submitted 2026-01-19, v2 2026-01-29). Verified quote: "structured per-class supervision yields stronger performance than percentage-based sampling," 150-shot MixPL (41.6 mAP) beats a COCO 5%-of-dataset SSOD baseline (40.1 mAP); on their specialized "Beetle" domain, MixPL hits 71.1 mAP by 100 shots/class "after which performance plateaus. Additional labeled data did not yield further improvements."

**Exact recommendation:** Before continuing the CVAT disagreement-queue push from 1,121 → 10-20k frames on current budget, insert explicit internal checkpoint evals at **1k / 3k / 6k / 10k reviewed rows** against the frozen held-out bar (F1@20px). If the curve is already flattening by 3-6k (as this paper's evidence suggests is common for specialized single-domain tasks), redirect surplus labeling budget toward court/lighting/venue diversity sampling instead of raw volume, or toward the coaching-wave label needs. This is a direct amendment to the P-series labeling-target task in NORTH_STAR — currently written as a flat 10-20k target with no interim decision gate.

**1b. Our own internal→held-out generalization gap (4 inversions, F1@20px 0.7248 never beaten) should be explicitly tracked as a "seen-environment vs unseen-environment" split, not just a single scalar bar.**
[CORROBORATED] — arxiv.org/abs/2603.06691 (v1 2026-03-04, v2 2026-03-17). Verified: YOLOv8 fine-tuned on 20,510 frames/11 backgrounds for egocentric shuttlecock detection scores F1=0.86 in similar-to-training environments vs F1=0.70 in entirely unseen environments (a ~16pt gap attributed to object size + background-texture shift), at a labeled-frame scale matching our own 10-20k target almost exactly.

**Exact recommendation:** Add an explicit "unseen-environment" stratum to `heldout_eval_ledger.md` (distinct courts/lighting/camera setups never touched during training or disagreement-mining) alongside the existing held-out row schema, and report F1@20px separately for seen-vs-unseen. This external result recalibrates what to expect: a 15-20pt seen/unseen gap at our label scale is normal for small racket-sport-object detection, not evidence the pipeline is broken — so the ledger should stop treating a single held-out number as the full picture and start tracking the gap itself as a first-class metric.

---

## (2) WHAT CONFIRMS OUR PLAN

**2a. Commodity RGB reprojection genuinely cannot reach usable spin accuracy at our noise floor — the actual unlock requires a sensing upgrade, not a better solver.**
[CORROBORATED] — arxiv.org/abs/2606.26780v1 (submitted 2026-06-25). Verified: an event camera + pan/tilt galvanometer mirrors + focus-tunable telephoto lens hits 2.1% magnitude / 4.0° axis error on static balls, and 8.8% magnitude / 6.4° axis error at 3ms latency / 750Hz throughput in live 3-view professional table-tennis. This is a **hardware-dependent** result (event sensor + actuated optics), not achievable from standard smartphone RGB.
**Read on our decision:** This directly supports the wave-5 honest kill of scalar-Magnus-spin (spin-on inflated reprojection RMSE on 2/3 clips at lambda=0.05, ≥8-inlier gate) and the frozen "gate on per-segment view-geometry confidence" ruling. No action needed — this is confirmatory evidence we killed the right thing for the right reason, and that further solver tuning (vs. a sensing upgrade we don't have) would not have closed the gap.

**2b. Our CVAT disagreement-queue design (large-offset / student-only / teacher-only buckets) mirrors validated active-learning literature and is not ad hoc.**
UNVERIFIED (not run through adversarial refute this pass, but pre-2025 and structurally uncontested) — arxiv.org/pdf/2308.08476 ("Classification Committee for Active Deep Object Detection"). Argues localization disagreement must be modeled alongside classification disagreement for effective detection active-learning — matches our "large-offset" bucket design. Treat as directional confirmation only; not independently re-verified this round.

**2c. The seed-trained student beating the 61k-public warm-start on hard frames is consistent with published "simple-label bias" theory.**
UNVERIFIED (not adversarially refuted) — arxiv.org/html/2507.00608v1 (DeSimPL, 2025-07). Proposes that self-labeling/large public corpora systematically over-represent easy/simple examples ("simple-label bias"), which — if true — mechanistically explains why raw tennis-ckpt zero-shot and the 61k-frame warm-start underperform our small, adversarially hard-mined (486-row) seed student on hard frames. Consistent with, but does not independently prove, the wave-3 owner ruling that killed the 2D-teacher plan in favor of raw WASB seed. Flag as supporting theory, not proof.

---

## (3) WATCH ITEMS (interesting, unverified)

**Detection/tracking architecture:**
- TrackNetV5 claims F1 0.9859 / accuracy 0.9733, a new SOTA beating TrackNetV4 by 2.78% F1 — UNVERIFIED overall (refute caught that the paper's own text never says "badminton"; it frames its target application as **tennis**, despite evaluating on the dataset conventionally called "TrackNetV2" which originates from badminton footage). Corrected framing: arxiv.org/html/2512.02789v1 (2025-12-02) — same-domain (non-pickleball) in-distribution benchmark either way; does not falsify our zero-shot cross-domain 0.7248 bar. Not urgent.
- TOTNet (occlusion-aware, 3D convs + visibility-weighted loss + occlusion augmentation; RMSE 37.30→7.19, occluded-frame accuracy 0.63→0.80) — UNVERIFIED overall; refute caught that the "no runtime numbers published" sub-claim is **false** — the paper's Table 2 reports ~28.08 FPS (TOTNet) / ~12.19 FPS (TOTNet-OF), 8.65-8.66M params. Corrected: arxiv.org/abs/2508.09650 (2025-08; journal version Computer Vision and Image Understanding, DOI 10.1016/j.cviu.2026.104657, Feb 2026). Occlusion-augmentation training recipe (independent of full architecture adoption) remains a plausible candidate to fold into our own seed-student training given occlusion is implicated in some of our inversions — but re-verify runtime/architecture claims directly before citing further.
- TrackNetV4 motion-attention-map fusion (arxiv.org/abs/2409.14543, 2025 ICASSP) — drop-in motion-difference channel on TrackNet backbones; not adversarially checked, pre-known-adjacent lineage.
- RF-DETR (Roboflow, ICLR 2026; github.com/roboflow/rf-detr) — DINOv2-backbone real-time DETR, no published F1@20px on a tennis/pickleball ball dataset; not a proven beat of our bar, just a bench candidate.
- Roboflow's May 2026 pickleball tutorial (RF-DETR + Claude Sonnet 4.5 commentary, 80.7% F1 general pickleball detection, no ball-tracking/trajectory work) — blog.roboflow.com/automate-pickleball-analytics, 2026-05-11. Below our ball-specific bar; relevant only as coaching-stage competitive color.

**Spin/trajectory:**
- TT3D (arxiv.org/html/2504.10035v2, 2025-04/06) — infers spin from **bounce-induced trajectory kinks**, not free-flight curvature; requires ≥3 visible frames post-bounce. Potentially a stronger identifiability cue than our killed free-flight-only Magnus fit, given pickleball's frequent bounces/paddle contacts. Worth a scoped look before any spin revisit — not yet verified.
- Synthetic-to-real spin classifier, zero real spin labels, 92.0% spin-class accuracy (arxiv.org/pdf/2504.19863, CVPRW 2025) — alternative to our least-squares physics solver; unverified.
- "Uplifting Table Tennis" (arxiv.org/abs/2511.20250, 2025-11-25) — synthetic-only 2D→3D+spin uplift net engineered for detector-noise robustness; PDF text not fully extractable, flagged for follow-up read.
- "Where Is The Ball" (arxiv.org/html/2506.05763v1, 2025-06) — explicitly skips spin/Magnus entirely, still gets 87.21% landing accuracy on tennis via gravity + ground-plane constraints only; weak indirect support for keeping spin non-load-bearing.
- TT4D (arxiv.org/html/2605.01234, 2026-05, ACM MM 2026 submission) — confirms the field still has no formal fps/segment-length/view-geometry identifiability threshold for monocular spin; supports continuing our own empirically-gated approach rather than searching for an external formula.

**Coaching / LLM grounding:**
- BioCoach (arxiv.org/abs/2603.26938, 2026-03-31) — 3-stage DOF-selector → structured biomechanical context → vision-conditioned feedback, architecturally near-identical to our planned 3-stage grounded coach. Worth a direct read of their DOF-scoping design.
- QEVD-bio-fit-coach benchmark + "LLM-Bio-Acc" judge metric (letsdatascience.com, 2026-06) — candidate eval-methodology pattern for our 0-fabrication bar; unverified, not primary-sourced directly.
- CHI 2026 survey (dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3772318.3791652) — documents ungrounded LLM sports feedback underperforming human coaches; directionally supports comparator-before-LLM design, not independently re-verified.
- RubricRAG (arxiv.org/pdf/2603.20882, 2026-03) — retrieval-grounded rubrics for LLM feedback constraint; candidate technique for the rule-comparator stage, unverified.
- PMC scoping review (ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12520646, 2025-2026) — no existing benchmark evaluates real-time corrective coaching adequacy; confirms we likely need our own eval harness.

**Competitive landscape (last full survey 2026-07-05; these are newer/unsurveyed names per that ruling):**
- PB Vision: $20/$50/mo tiers, "3D trajectory analysis" = shot-path-level, not full-scene 3D; DUPR rating-integration partnership (softwarefinder.com/artificial-intelligence/pb-vision). Unverified this pass.
- SwingVision: AI coaching bundled in Pro tier, already ships post-session coaching advice, appears stat-summary-based not fabrication-audited (swing.vision). Unverified this pass.
- SwingVantage "Motion Lab": free phone-only 3D body-motion reconstruction with phase scores, pickleball-specific (swingvantage.com/pickleball). Load-bearing competitive signal if accurate — closest existing product to our "3D" claim; our differentiation would need to rest on full-scene (ball+court+multi-player) fidelity and trust bands, not "3D" alone. Unverified this pass — recommend a direct hands-on check before using this to reposition messaging.
- Wingfield: hardware SKU, ~€6,999 one-time or ~€187/mo financing (wingfield.io/en/products). Different business model, informs a possible venue-hardware pricing anchor only.
- PlayReplay (via Pickleball Inc. distribution): 4-camera line-calling, ITF Silver Classification, 100M+ calls/250k+ matches logged, also ball speed/spin/shot-type (playreplay.io news release). Benchmark for "mature" ball-physics data volume vs our 1,121 frames.
- Owl AI + MLP: broadcast-camera-only AI officiating, live 2026 Dallas season (sportsvideo.org, 2026-05-22). Officiating segment, low direct relevance; useful "software-only, existing-camera" messaging parallel.

---

## (4) DEAD ENDS SEARCHED

- paperswithcode.com's sports-ball-detection-and-tracking-on-tennis leaderboard page redirected to an unrelated page — no independent leaderboard corroboration obtained beyond what the TrackNetV4/V5/TOTNet papers self-report.
- No paper found benchmarking directly against "WASB-SBDT" by name, nor any claiming cross-sport zero-shot transfer comparable to our own WASB-on-pickleball setup — the domain-transfer angle we care about most remains unaddressed in the literature.
- "Active learning disagreement sampling object detection domain adaptation 2026" search surfaced only pre-2025 general-AL background and adjacent-but-mismatched hits (Bi3D, DELTA, source-free ADA); no 2026 racket/ball-sport-specific disagreement-AL result.
- TrackNet-lineage search returned no new 2025-2026 domain-adaptation paper beyond already-known TrackNetV4.
- WebFetch of arxiv PDF 2409.09412 ("Label Convergence... Contradictory Annotations," potentially relevant to our F1@20px ceiling/inversions) returned corrupted binary content — needs retry via the abstract/HTML page, not the raw PDF.
- Leave-one-domain-out generalization search returned only generic PACS/OfficeHome/DomainNet benchmarks — no sports/ball-tracking LOSO result newer or more specific than our own standing LoSO practice.
- Direct WebFetch of arxiv PDF binaries for 2511.20250 and 2504.19863 (pdf variant) failed to parse — quantitative spin-on/spin-off ablation numbers for "Uplifting Table Tennis" remain unconfirmed pending a direct read via the HTML mirror.
- No paper found with a direct spin-on-vs-spin-off reprojection-error ablation matching our exact experiment shape (TT3D comes closest but doesn't quantify this either).
- Event-based gaze-control spin paper was noted but not deep-dived on hardware-adoption feasibility, given it requires specialized (non-smartphone) optics — out of scope for our pipeline as-is.
- General RAG hallucination-mitigation literature is generic factuality work, not sports/coaching-specific — confirms direction, no sport-specific technique to borrow.
- AgentCoach, SoleCoach, BoxingPro, GPTCoach (CHI 2026 / IoT-LLM coaching systems) are motivational/adherence or wearable-sensor systems, not vision/3D-pose-grounded technique coaching — adjacent, not closer matches than BioCoach.
- Rubric-generation-for-LLM-judge papers (Auto-Rubric, OptimSyn, FeedEval, iRULER) are general NLG/essay-feedback grounding work, not sports-specific — RubricRAG banked, rest are dead ends.
- No published processing-latency figures found for pb.vision, SwingVision, Wingfield, PlayReplay, or Owl AI — all qualitative "fast turnaround" claims only.
- No public px-level or F1-level accuracy claim found for any competitor's ball-tracking beyond PlayReplay's ITF Silver Classification and "100M+ calls" scale claim — nothing directly comparable to our F1@20px bar.
- No competitor found publicly claiming a full multi-body + ball + court joint 3D scene reconstruction with trust/confidence bands (our core claim) — SwingVantage Motion Lab is closest but appears single-player, body-only.
- No new entrants found beyond SwingVantage/Wingfield/PlayReplay/Owl AI since the 2026-07-05 survey; a Twitter/X or Product Hunt pass might surface earlier-stage/stealth entrants not yet web-indexed.
